definitions.definition: to allow time to go by, especially while staying in one place without doing very much, until someone comes, until something that you are expecting happens or until you can do something

examples: Cider is poured over the roots of the tree and the wassailers circle the tree chanting: Stand fast root, bear well top.

definitions.definition: very much, to a great degree, or completely
examples: Knead the dough well, then divide it into four pieces.
He could well imagine how much his promise was going to cost him.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize some prepositions
examples: The results are well above/below what we expected.
Keep well away from the edge of the cliff.
It cost well over £100.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize some adjectives
examples: The police are well aware of the situation.
The museum is well worth a visit.
Some machines look more like cheap, plastic toys - leave these well alone.

definitions.definition: very
examples: The film was well good.

definitions.definition: with good reason
examples: She might well be the best person to ask.

definitions.definition: in addition (to)
examples: Invite Emily - and Scott as well.

definitions.definition: healthy; not ill

definitions.definition: used to introduce something you are going to say, often to show surprise, doubt, slight disagreement, or anger, or to continue a story
examples: Well, what shall we do now?
Well now/then, how are we going to arrange things?

"He's decided to give up his job and move to Seattle with her." "Well, well - that's what love does for you."

Well, after that we went camping in the mountains.

definitions.definition: what/which time
examples: "Did you know Lucy was back in England?" "Is she - since when?"

definitions.definition: considering the fact that
examples: How can you say you don't like something when you've never even tried it!
You can't complain about being lonely when you don't make any effort to meet people.
Why is she training to be a teacher when she doesn't even like children?

definitions.definition: despite the fact that
examples: He says he doesn't have any money when in fact he has thousands of dollars in his account.

definitions.definition: (from) where

definitions.definition: in what situation
examples: You're not available on the 12th and Andrew can't make the 20th - so where does that leave us?

definitions.definition: by which way or method

definitions.definition: in which, or with which

definitions.definition: for what reason; why

examples: The present crisis is the result of years of wilful neglect by the council.

definitions.definition: used to talk about what is going to happen in the future, especially things that you are certain about or things that are planned
examples: Clare will be five years old next month.
The train leaves at 8:58, so we'll be in Scotland by lunchtime.
I'll see him tomorrow./I'll be seeing him tomorrow.
Will Susie be there?
It won't be easy to find another secretary.
There'll be trouble when she finds out.

definitions.definition: used to talk about what someone or something is able or willing to do
examples: I'll give you a lift.
Ask Gabriela if she'll take them.
I've asked her but she won't come.
The car won't start.
This lasagne will feed six people.

definitions.definition: used to ask someone to do something
examples: Will you give me her address?
Will you give that to Tony when you see him, please?

definitions.definition: used as a polite way of inviting someone to do something, or of offering someone something
examples: Will you join us for a cup of coffee, Evie?
Will you come in for a while?
You'll have some cake, won't you, Charlie?

definitions.definition: used in conditional sentences with 'if'
examples: If he's late again, I'll be very angry.
I'll wait with Christopher if his mother isn't here when you go.

definitions.definition: used to refer to what is likely
examples: That'll be Scott at the door.
That'll be his mother with him.
As you all will know, election day is next week.

definitions.definition: used when angry to tell someone to do something
examples: Will you stop being such a pain!
You'll go upstairs and you'll go straight to bed like your father told you!

definitions.definition: used when referring to something that always or usually happens
examples: Accidents will happen.
Fruit will keep longer in the fridge.
The product with the better-known brand name will always sell better.
She's 85 now, but she will insist on doing all her own housework.

definitions.definition: the mental power used to control and direct your thoughts and actions, or a determination to do something, despite any difficulties or opposition
examples: From an early age she had a very strong will.

definitions.definition: what someone wants to happen
examples: It was God's will.

examples: I wish (that) I was/were a bit taller.

definitions.definition: used with the past perfect to express that you feel sorry or sad about a particular action in the past
examples: I wish (that) I hadn't eaten so much.
I bet she wishes (that) she'd never got involved in the whole mess.

definitions.definition: to want to do something
examples: As you wish, Sir. It will be done.
I wish to make a complaint.
Passengers wishing to take the Kings Cross train should board now.
I don't wish to worry you but he did say he'd be back by midnight.
Sometimes I was so depressed that I wished myself dead.

definitions.definition: used to express anger at someone's behaviour
examples: I wish she'd shut up for a minute and let someone else talk.
I wish you'd look at me when I'm trying to talk to you!

definitions.definition: to hope or express hope for another person's success or happiness or pleasure on a particular occasion
examples: We wish you every success in the future.
I didn't even see her to wish her a happy birthday/wish a happy birthday to her.
I wished her a safe journey and waved her off.

definitions.definition: to hope someone will succeed
examples: I wished him well with his new venture.

definitions.definition: to hope that something you want will be made real because of good luck or magical powers
examples: I remember blowing out the candles on my birthday cake and wishing that John Lee would be my boyfriend.
If I could wish myself anywhere in the world (= go anywhere as a result of making a wish) right now it would be somewhere hot and sunny.

"By the time I'm 40, I'll be rich." "You wish!" (= There is no chance of that happening.)

definitions.definition: something you say or write at the end of a letter, etc. to show that you hope someone is happy and has good luck

definitions.definition: a hope that is made real with magical powers

definitions.definition: using something

definitions.definition: having or including something

definitions.definition: including

examples: She laid the table with the best china.

She filled the jug up with cream.

definitions.definition: used with words showing separation
examples: I'd rather not part with my cash.

definitions.definition: and, or followed by

definitions.definition: used in comparisons
examples: I have nothing in common with my brother.

definitions.definition: supporting someone or something

definitions.definition: in the same direction as something
examples: The wind was with me on the home stretch and I ran well.

definitions.definition: at the same rate or time as something
examples: This wine will improve with age.

definitions.definition: to understand what someone is saying
examples: You look puzzled - are you with me?

definitions.definition: despite something
examples: With all her faults, she's still a really good friend.

definitions.definition: used to express a wish or instruction
examples: Away with you! (= Go away!)

definitions.definition: in addition
examples: He was a man of great means, and of great intellect withal.

definitions.definition: in spite of what has been said
examples: It was, withal, a carefully crafted speech.

definitions.definition: to take or move out or back, or to remove
examples: This credit card allows you to withdraw up to £200 a day from ATMs.

definitions.definition: when you take money out of a bank account

definitions.definition: the process or action of a military force moving out of an area
examples: The commander-in-chief was given 36 hours to secure a withdrawal of his troops from the combat zone.

definitions.definition: the act or process of taking something away so that it is no longer available, or of someone stopping being involved in an activity

examples: He signed the treaty in the presence of two witnesses.

definitions.definition: a person who is allowed to give their opinion in a law court because of their knowledge or practical experience of a particular subject

definitions.definition: to show or give proof of something
examples: This year's charity ball was the most successful one ever, as witnessed by the number of tickets sold.

Roads in rural areas were worst affected by the snow.
The students voted him the school's worst-dressed teacher.

definitions.definition: used to emphasize what is worst about a situation
examples: We had no food or light, but worst of all, we had no water.

definitions.definition: to defeat someone in a fight or argument
examples: He was worsted in a fight with a bigger boy.

definitions.definition: a liquid produced from the grain when making beer or whiskey
used in the name of some plants, especially some that are or have been used in herbal medicine
examples: motherworts

definitions.definition: having a particular value, especially in money
examples: Our house is worth £200,000.
Heroin worth about $5 million was seized.

definitions.definition: having a particular amount of money
examples: She must be worth at least half a million.

definitions.definition: to be of reasonable or good value for the price
examples: Four days' car hire costs £150, which is well worth it for the freedom it gives you.

definitions.definition: to be important or interesting enough to receive a particular action
examples: I think this matter is worth our attention.
When you're in Reykjavík, the National Museum is worth a visit.

definitions.definition: to be important or useful to have or do
examples: There's nothing worth reading in this newspaper.
If you are a young, inexperienced driver, it is worth having comprehensive insurance.
It's worth remembering that prices go up in February.

definitions.definition: enjoyable enough or producing enough advantages to make the necessary effort, risk, pain, etc. seem acceptable
examples: It was a long climb to the top of the hill, but it was worth it for the view from the top.
Don't tire yourself out, Geri, it's really not worth it.
After the plastic surgery I had two black eyes and my face was very swollen. But I knew it would be worth it.
Forget him, sweetheart, - he's just not worth it.

definitions.definition: the amount of money that something can be sold for; value
examples: The estimated worth of the plastics and petrochemical industry is about $640 billion.

definitions.definition: the amount of something that you could buy for £20, $100, etc.
examples: $4 million worth of souvenirs and gift items have been produced for the event.

definitions.definition: the importance or usefulness of something or someone
examples: He felt as though he had no worth.
She has proved her worth on numerous occasions.
The study proved that women were paid less than men holding jobs of comparable worth.

definitions.definition: an amount of something that will last a month, a year, etc. or that takes a month, a year, etc. to do
examples: a month's worth of grocery shopping
I've done three hour's worth of work this morning.

definitions.definition: deserving respect, admiration, or support
examples: He is unlikely to succeed in getting his bill through Congress, however worthy it is.
Every year she makes a large donation to a worthy cause.

definitions.definition: deserving to be given attention, noticed, etc.
examples: Two points in this report are especially worthy of notice.

definitions.definition: Something that is worthy is not very interesting but should be admired for its good and useful qualities
examples: a worthy book

definitions.definition: suitable for or characteristic of something
examples: He threw a party worthy of a millionaire.

definitions.definition: a person who is important, especially in a small town
examples: The front row of chairs was reserved for local worthies.

definitions.definition: suitable or deserving to receive a particular thing
examples: trustworthy
creditworthy
newsworthy

definitions.definition: used to describe a boat, aircraft, or vehicle that is suitable to be used safely in a particular substance or environment
examples: seaworthy
roadworthy

definitions.definition: used to refer to future time from the point of view of the past
examples: He said he would see his brother tomorrow.

definitions.definition: used to refer back to a time in the past from a point of view in the future
examples: We thought they would have got home by five o'clock, but there was no reply when we phoned.

definitions.definition: used to refer to an intention from the point of view of the past
examples: He said he would always love her .
They promised that they would help.
There was nobody left who would (= was willing to) do it.
I asked him to move his car but he said he wouldn't (= he refused).

definitions.definition: used to refer to a situation that you can imagine happening
examples: I would hate to miss the show.

definitions.definition: used with if in conditional sentences (= sentences that refer to what happens if something else happens)
examples: What would you do if you lost your job?

definitions.definition: used in polite requests and offers
examples: Would you mind sharing a room?
Would you like me to come with you?
Would you like some cake?

definitions.definition: past simple of will used to talk about what someone was willing to do or what something was able to do
examples: The car wouldn't start this morning.

definitions.definition: used to talk about things in the past that happened often or always
examples: He would always turn and wave at the end of the street.

definitions.definition: used to suggest that what happens is expected because it is typical, especially of a person's behaviour
examples: "Madeleine called to say she's too busy to come." "She would - she always has an excuse."

definitions.definition: used to express an opinion in a polite way without being forceful

definitions.definition: used after "I" when giving advice
examples: I wouldn't (= I advise you not to) worry about it, if I were you.

definitions.definition: used after "why" when giving or asking the reason for something
examples: Why would anyone want to eat something so horrible?

definitions.definition: used to refer to what is very likely
examples: "The guy on the phone had a Southern accent." "That would be Tom."

examples: You were wrong about the time - the bank closed at 3.30.

definitions.definition: to show by your actions that someone's judgment of you was not correct
examples: I thought she couldn't do it, but she proved me wrong.

definitions.definition: not suitable or correct, or not as it should be
examples: She's the wrong person for the job.

definitions.definition: Something that is wrong is not considered to be socially acceptable or suitable
examples: She got in with the wrong crowd (= a group of people who behaved badly) at university.

definitions.definition: If you ask someone what is wrong, you want to know what is worrying or upsetting them
examples: You've been quiet all evening. Is there anything wrong?
What's wrong with you today?

definitions.definition: If something is the wrong way around, the part that should be at the front is at the back
examples: You've got your skirt on the wrong way round.

definitions.definition: not considered morally acceptable by most people
examples: Children should be taught that violence is wrong.

definitions.definition: not working correctly
examples: Something's wrong with the television - the picture's fuzzy.

definitions.definition: in a way that is not correct

definitions.definition: to make a mistake in the way you answer or understand something
examples: I spent hours doing that problem and I still got the answer wrong.

definitions.definition: If a situation or event goes wrong, it becomes unpleasant and is not a success

definitions.definition: to make a mistake
